original_abstract: This study employs high-resolution mass spectrometry to investigate the dramatic acceleration of chemical and biochemical reactions within charged microdroplets compared to traditional bulk solutions. We examined two distinct processes: the Pomeranz–Fritsch synthesis of isoquinoline, involving covalent bond formation, and the noncovalent complexation between cytochrome c and maltose. Our results demonstrate that the reaction rate for isoquinoline synthesis is increased by a factor of over one million, while the protein-ligand binding occurs more than a thousand times faster than in bulk water. While the exact physical mechanisms underlying this phenomenon remain under investigation, analysis of droplet fusion suggests that reagents are concentrated and confined within a small volume (0.5 pl – 2 nl). Experimental observations of fused aqueous droplets indicate that significant evaporation does not occur during the relevant flight time, implying that the droplet–air interface likely plays a critical role in catalyzing these processes. These findings suggest that microdroplet chemistry offers a powerful and general platform for bypassing the kinetic limitations of slow or difficult reactions. Furthermore, this approach provides a unique environment for studying molecular interactions under confinement, potentially mirroring the crowded conditions found in biological cells.
humanized_abstract: Using high-resolution mass spectrometry, we investigated the conditions under which charged microdroplets could induce dramatic acceleration of chemical and biochemical transformations relative to their bulk solutions. We probed two very different reaction scenarios, involving the covalent formation of bonds (Pomeranz–Fritsch synthesis of isoquinoline) and the noncovalent binding of a protein by its ligand (cytochrome c/maltose complex). For both cases, the acceleration factors with respect to the aqueous bulk solution were estimated to exceed one million and one thousand, respectively. Analyzing the impact of the fusion of microdroplets allows us to concentrate reactants in a very small volume (0.5 pl – 2 nl), while observations of the corresponding fused aqueous droplets suggest that little evaporation occurs during the corresponding time of flight. This therefore strongly suggests that the droplet–air interface is a critical component for facilitating the acceleration of the catalysed reactions, and that microdroplet chemistry is a very efficient general method for bypassing slow reaction kinetics barriers associated with slow or poor catalysed reactions. It should also provide a very new and unique environment to study the effect of molecular crowding, which is present in almost all biological systems.

Analyzing the impact of the fusion of microdroplets allows us to concentrate reactants in a very small volume (0.5 pl – 2 nl), while observations of the corresponding fused aqueous droplets suggest that little evaporation occurs during the corresponding time of flight. This therefore strongly suggests that the droplet–air interface is a critical component for facilitating the acceleration of the catalysed reactions, and that microdroplet chemistry is a very efficient general method for bypassing slow reaction kinetics barriers associated with slow or poor catalysed reactions.
